statusLine: WTI crude trades at $94.25/bbl as US inventories decline 8 million barrels while global shipping routes face persistent security threats.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets show tightening fundamentals as commercial inventories fell by 8.0 million barrels in the week ending June 4, 2026, bringing stocks to 357.1 million barrels. This drawdown occurred alongside sustained domestic production at 13.7 million barrels per day, indicating strong demand absorption. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve remains at 83.3 million barrels, well below historical norms and limiting federal buffer capacity.

WTI crude declined 2.19% week-on-week to $94.25/bbl in the June 4 reporting period, trading at a $1.59 discount to Brent crude. This modest discount reflects adequate US Gulf Coast export capacity despite global supply route disruptions. Retail fuel prices provided some consumer relief, with gasoline falling 16.6 cents to $4.44/gallon and diesel dropping 17.3 cents to $5.35/gallon, though both remain elevated by historical standards.

Critical maritime security alerts continue across key global chokepoints, with Houthi attacks targeting commercial vessels in the Red Sea and Bab el Mandeb Strait, while Iranian threats persist in the Strait of Hormuz. These disruptions force longer shipping routes and higher insurance costs, supporting elevated crude prices despite adequate Americas supply fundamentals.

Across the broader Americas, production growth from Guyana's Stabroek block continues ramping while Brazil maintains robust pre-salt output. Canada's oil sands face ongoing pipeline capacity constraints, keeping Western Canadian Select at traditional discounts to WTI. Venezuela's production remains constrained under sanctions, limiting regional supply growth potential.
keyPoints: US crude inventories fell 8.0 million barrels to 357.1 million in the week ending June 4, indicating strong demand
WTI declined 2.19% week-on-week to $94.25/bbl while trading at $1.59 discount to Brent
Critical maritime security threats persist in Red Sea and Persian Gulf shipping lanes
US production steady at 13.7 million bpd with Strategic Petroleum Reserve at 83.3 million barrels
Retail gasoline and diesel prices declined but remain elevated at $4.44 and $5.35 per gallon respectively
Global shipping disruptions continue supporting crude prices despite adequate Americas supply
